system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 7: Jazz Standard Study #6: So What
Pages 102-115 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

There is a specific reason why | want to talk about the jazz standard
So What. Of all the songs we have covered and will cover in this book,
this is by far the most harmonically simple one. In fact, there are only
two chords in the entire song.

So What is one of the most classic examples of modal harmony. This
means that the harmonic language is based on the modes of a given
key center. |’ll explain more about modes in the upcoming pages.

Modal harmony is not based on “functional harmony.” So far in this
book, every jazz standard has used harmonic movement based on ei-
ther the Major or Minor Diatonic Series of 7“ chords. We have seen ii-
V-| chord progressions, and I-vi-ii-V chord progressions, among others,
all of which are derived from functional harmony.

We'll talk more about this when we examine the chords analysis, but
| want to make clear that the reason we are studying this song is be-
cause it should be approached differently than the others we have

worked on.
Se What Bie

So What was written by Miles Davis in 1959 for the iconic album “Kind of Blue.”
Miles Davis was the pioneer of modal jazz music and So What may be one of
the first examples of this. The tune has an AABA form and is based on the Dorian
mode of two different key centers.

In my opinion, the only recording you really need to listen to is the origi-
nal from “Kind of Blue.” There is so much rich musical knowledge to be
gained from this recording and this entire album in general.

In the Suggested Listening this time around, | am also suggesting you
listen to a song called Impressions by John Coltrane. Impressions is
a contrafact of So What, as it shares the exact same chord changes
with a different melody.


| suggest listening to and learning this one as well because the chord
changes to So What are relatively simple. It can be helpful to know
some different heads to play over this form.

>> SUGGESTED LISTENING

Miles Davis from “Kind of Blue” 1959

“Impressions” John Coltrane from “Impressions”
1961

Because So What is based on modal harmony, there is no need to
identify the guide tones. This is because there is little to no harmon-
ic movement actually happening. There are no chords to connect to-
gether (other than one key change), and because the best way to im-
provise over modal harmony is by using modes, guide tones aren’t so
necessary.

So, the proceeding pages will include the chord map and chords anal-
ysis. We will be forgoing the guide tones chart this time around.

Note that on the chord map there are chordal hits included. This is
based off of the arrangement as heard on “Kind of Blue.” Be sure to
listen to that recording so that you fully understand what is going on.


SO WHAT

SO WHAT
Chords Analysis

As always, let’s go over the color coding:

Notice on the analysis that in parenthesis | include the parent key. This
iS important to understand because when we talk about the Dorian
minor mode, it really means that we are playing within a major key. If
you don’t understand how modes work, this next section is for you.

A CRASH COURSE QN HOW MODES WORK

| do cover modes quite thoroughly in my other book Zero to Improv,
but it’s important for me to repeat some of that information here so
you can fully understand So What and how the harmony works.

>> WHAT IS A MODE?

A mode is a type of scale. ‘Mode’ comes from the Latin for ‘manner or
method’ but the names of them are Greek because that's where they
originated from. Each mode is related to its parent major scale (Ionian).
You can think of each mode as starting and finishing on a given tone of
the parent scale.

The easiest way for me to describe modes is to think of them as scales
starting and ending on different tones of their parent scale. There are
7 modes (one for each scale tone of the major scale) to be aware of.

I’m going to list them out starting with the parent key of the first chord
of So What.

107


IONIAN (PARENT MAJOR SCALE)

Intervallic formula: W-W-H-W-W-W-H
Scale tone formula: 1-2-3-4-5-6-7
C lonian: C-D-E-F-G-A-B

Intervallic formula: W-H-W-W-W-H-W
Scale tone formula: 1-2-b3-4-5-6-b7
D Dorian: D-E-F-G-A-B-C

PHRYGIAN

Intervallic formula: H-W-W-W-H-W-W
Scale tone formula: 1-b2-b3-4-5-b6-b7
E Phrygian: E-F-G-A-B-C-D

Intervallic formula: W-W-W-H-W-W-H
Scale tone formula: 1-2-3-#4-5-6-7
F Lydian: F-G-A-B-C-D-E

MIXOLYDIAN

Intervallic formula: W-W-H-W-W-H-W
Scale tone formula: 1-2-3-4-5-6-b7
G Mixolydian: G-A-B-C-D-E-F


Intervallic formula: W-H-W-W-H-W-W
Scale tone formula: 1-2-b3-4-5-b6-b7
A Aeolian: A-B-C-D-E-F-G

Intervallic formula: H-W-W-H-W-W-W
Scale tone formula: 1-b2-b3-4-b5-b6-b7
B Loerian: B-C-D-E-F-G-A

For each of these modes, I’ve listed an intervallic formula. The “W”
stands for whole step, and the “H” stands for half step.

The scale tone formula demonstrates how you would construct the
mode from the root note on up.

Finally, | include the notes for each mode so you can see this applied.
It’s important for you to understand that each of these modes are
related to the lonian parent major scale. If you look at Lydian for ex-
ample, you can see that it just starts and ends on the 4* tone of the
parent major scale. Each mode is constructed from the lonian parent
major scale, just starting at different points of the scale. If you learn
to understand modes as in relation to their parent major scale, the
concept becomes much easier to digest.

For So What, the mode we are concerned about is the Dorian mode.

DORIAN MODE

@ fz f

o @ .

THT?

In this case we are dealing with D Dorian. So what is the parent major
scale, or lonian, of D Dorian? C major. So a D Dorian is simply a C ma-
jor scale starting and ending on the 2" scale degree.



Now if you look back at the chords analysis, you will notice there is a
key center change from bars 9-16.

For these brief 8 bars it moves to Eb Dorian, which means the parent
key is Db major. This is just a half step up from the previous key center.
So the two parent keys we are dealing with in So What are C major
and Db major.

EPuin7
* Eb Dorian (Parent Key Db Major) - - - - --------------------------------------

EPain?

The harmony in So What is reasonably simple, as long as you under-
stand the modes that are being dealt with and their parent key cen-

ters.

THE JAZZ STANDARDS PLAYBOOK [0 |



Recorded 8va

IMPROV LESSON
Using the Medes te Improvise

This Improv Lesson is going to be structured similarly to the last in that
we will start with some exercises and then move to a more musical
application.

| have already mentioned that to improvise over So What you can use
the Dorian mode of each appropriate parent scale. But since all of the
modes are related to the parent scale, you can technically use them
all to improvise.

As |’ve mentioned already with scales, this doesn’t mean you should
play the scales like scales. You should just use them as note choices,
and sometimes thinking of them in the context of different modes can
be helpful.

Let’s start with a simple exercise that plays the arpeggios of each
chord in the Major Diatonic Series. That’s right. Since the diatonic se-
ries is merely 7“ chords harmonized with the major scale, that means
that each chord in the series is related to a mode. Take a look and play
through this.

MODAL ARPEGGIO EXERCISE

In this exercise, | have connected each arpeggio with the nearest chord
tone of the proceeding chord. As you'll notice, this means the arpeggio
of each chord doesn’t always start on the root. But that’s a good thing
because you should be able to play a chord starting on any chord tone.

Here’s something for you to try: jam along to So What either using the
play-along | provided for you in your download folder or have someone
else accompany you. You will notice that playing this exercise fits per-
fectly over the first 16 bars. Each arpeggio in the series has a different
flavor, but they are still using notes from the parent major scale. You
can use arpeggios in the diatonic series by treating them as modes in
the context of So What.

Now let’s do another exercise, but we'll go a little bit further and fill
in the blanks. This time | have constructed a basic pattern using the
modes and moving through the series.

MODAL PATTERN EXERCISE

Ionian Dorian Phrygian Lydian

OF = = @ 17 SS @

Mixolydian Aeolian Locrian Ionian

ae Se See Se ea sa ee ee eases

We are using a descending and ascending pattern connecting the
modes together. In this case, the pattern moves in 3rds and connects
to the next mode in either a half step or a whole step. Play through this
so you can get it under your fingers.

The idea of these exercises is to help you become familiar with the
modes, and understand that all of them are connected. Armed with
this knowledge, you don’t have to feel boxed in by the parent major
scale or the Dorian mode when improvising over songs like So What.
The key is learning how to use them properly and musically.



Now let’s move on to something more musical.

I want you to stop what you are doing now and go listen to Miles
Davis’ solo on So What from “Kind of Blue.”

Miles is famous for a lot of things, but one thing that | particularly ad-
mire is he is the master of understatement. If we want to understand
how to improvise over So What, what better thing to do than listen to
how Miles did it? Find the recording using the Amazon link | provided in
the Suggested Recordings, look it up on YouTube or however you like
to purchase your music.

| originally wanted to share with you a transcription of the first chorus
of Miles’ solo, but considering there are copyright complications in-
volved in that, | decided to pass. | would encourage you to go out on
your own and learn the first chorus of that solo.

But what | have decided to do instead | think is an even better assign-
ment. On the next page, | composed a chorus of So What in the style
of Miles Davis. | have listened carefully to how Miles improvised on this
song and have done my best to replicate the essence of his approach.

Here are some characteristics | noted:

v He uses lots of space. He doesn’t feel the need to play lots of
notes.

¥ He plays a phrase, leaves some space, and then expands upon
that phrase.

v¥ He generally plays within the Dorian mode, with exceptions of
course and occasional chromatic approach notes and such.

Take a look and play through this chorus | composed.


SO WHAT CHORUS IN THE STYLE OF MILES

Work on playing my solo and see if it helos you make any connections
between the use of the Dorian mode and Miles’ style. You may have
also noticed | repeat a lot of patterns that move more or less in 3rds
which we worked on in the previous exercises.

My challenge to you is to start jamming over So What and try to
improvise using the Dorian and other modes in the style of Miles.
You don’t need to compose a solo, just improvise to the best of your
ability. You don’t need to be an experienced player, and it doesn’t have
to sound perfect.

The only way you can start becoming a better improviser is just to do
it. You can read books about it, learn scales, transcribe solos, but at
the end of the day, you have to go get your hands dirty.

So What is an important tune to know, and it is the best introduction
to modal harmony which you will come across in other jazz standards.
Be sure to spend some time with this song and get to know it well.

Let’s move on to our next jazz standard study: On Green Dolphin Street.


CHAPTER TEXT>>>
